text: Good evening. Welcome to your daily reflection. Let's look at your day.
How would you describe today generally?

options.label: Productive and largely went as expected
I adjusted to some unexpected challenges
Frustrating and chaotic
Mostly dealing with other people's messes

text: You described today as '{A1_OPEN.answer_label}'. That's heavy. When days feel that heavy, our brains naturally focus on what went wrong or what we couldn't control. That's not a flaw—it's protection.
During a moment that went well today, what drove that positive outcome?

options.label: I anticipated what was needed and prepared well
I adapted my approach when priorities shifted
The environment or timing was favorable
My team handled the hardest parts

text: When things got difficult, what was your initial instinct?

options.label: I looked for what I could control and salvaged it
I figured out a workaround to keep moving
I waited for the person responsible to fix it
I felt stuck because the system is broken

text: Look at what you just told me: you said '{A1_Q_AGENCY_L.answer_label}'. You're taking responsibility for navigating the day—which is good. But notice if you're taking responsibility for *everything that went wrong*. Not everything is on you. Tomorrow, try distinguishing between 'things I could influence' and 'things I needed to accept.'

You chose '{A1_Q_AGENCY_H.answer_label}'. You're seeing your hand in how today played out. Even when things weren't perfect, you stayed in the driver's seat. That's a strong internal locus of control.
A tough day pulls attention outward—to the system, the chaos, or what others did to create drag. Somewhere in there, though, you made a choice. That's where your agency lies.
Now let's shift from how much agency you felt today—to what you gave and expected in return.
Think about an interaction you had today regarding work distribution or recognition. How did it feel?

options.label: I did work that went largely unacknowledged
I felt annoyed that I carry more weight than some others
I stepped in to help someone without being asked
I focused on getting my deliverables over the line

text: You mentioned feeling '{A2_OPEN.answer_label}'. When you think about what you deserve at work, where does that feeling come from?

options.label: I work harder than most, so I expect better treatment or recognition
I feel the organization owes me for the sacrifices I make
I try to remind myself that recognition is earned, not guaranteed
I realize my frustration comes from wanting my contribution to matter

text: You chose '{A2_OPEN.answer_label}'. Which of these describes why you took that approach?

options.label: Because building up the team makes all of our work better
Because the work simply needed to get done
Because I wanted leadership to notice my extra effort
Because it's unfair if I have to do it and others don't

text: You chose '{A2_OPEN.answer_label}' — and your rationale was '{A2_Q_CONTRIB.answer_label}'. That's the definition of contribution. You are focusing on what you can give to the system and the team, rather than waiting to see what the system will give you.

It's natural to track what's 'fair'—especially when you work hard. But measuring what you feel owed can create a subtle sense of entitlement, draining your energy if reality doesn't match your expectations.
Finally, let's zoom out from your specific tasks and expectations, and look at the radius of your concern today.
When you think about the most stressful or significant moment of your day, who was at the center of the narrative?

options.label: Just me—it was something I had to endure or achieve
Me and my immediate manager/colleague
The broader team and our collective goals
The customer, end-user, or the ultimate mission

text: It makes sense that you felt wrapped up in that moment. What would happen if you adopted a wider perspective on that same issue?

options.label: I'd struggle, honestly; I'm too stressed by my own constraints
I feel my problems need to be solved before I can worry about others
I might see that my colleague is under similar pressure
I'd realign my priorities with what's actually best for the project

text: Thinking beyond yourself takes energy. What enables you to hold a wider radius of concern like '{A3_OPEN.answer_label}'?

options.label: I find a deeper sense of meaning when I connect my work to others
I recognize we only succeed or fail as a unit
Honestly, I do it because it looks good to leadership
I feel pressured to care about others even when I'm burnt out

text: Your radius of concern is wide. By holding others in view, you transcend immediate self-interest. This altrocentric mindset often dilutes personal stress because you're connected to something larger.

When we operate highly self-centrically, every small friction feels like a personal threat. Notice tomorrow if widening your lens—asking 'What does the team need?'—actually lightens your personal burden.
When you started this session, you were focused on '{A1_OPEN.answer_label}'. By the end, you're considering '{A3_OPEN.answer_label}'. That's movement. That's reflection working.

syntheses.narrative: You see your choices. You give without keeping score. And you think about the team and the mission. That's rare. That's the person everyone wants to work with.
syntheses.edge_case: The trap: you might burn out by giving too much. Tomorrow, notice: are you building something, or just filling holes others left?
syntheses.forward: Stay curious about what you're building, not just what you're fixing.

syntheses.narrative: Today felt like the world was working against you. And when that happens, we tighten up—focus on what we're not getting instead of what we could build.
syntheses.edge_case: This isn't permanent. It's a mood, not your character.
syntheses.forward: Tomorrow, start with one small choice you know you can make. See if it opens things up.

syntheses.narrative: You see your choices, you care about the mission—but today it felt like your effort went unnoticed. That gap between effort and recognition stings.
syntheses.edge_case: Real question: are you doing this work for the team, or for the recognition? Both are human. But the answer shapes everything else.
syntheses.forward: Test: tomorrow, do something extra for the mission, not for the credit. Notice what that feels like.

syntheses.narrative: You navigated today well. You gave something. But you're still holding it pretty close—'my work', 'my effort', 'my impact'. Nothing wrong with that. But there's more available if you open the circle.
syntheses.edge_case: You're not selfish. You're just not yet thinking beyond your own work.
syntheses.forward: Tomorrow, notice how your work ripples into others' days. That awareness is the beginning of something bigger.

syntheses.narrative: Even when things felt out of your hands today, you still showed up for others and the mission. That takes intense resilience.
syntheses.edge_case: Be careful of martyrdom. You can give a lot without accepting that you are powerless.
syntheses.forward: Tomorrow, find one area where you can legally say 'no' to take back some boundaries.

syntheses.narrative: You are a driver, but your drive is heavily focused inward right now. You know you can execute, but you're intensely focused on the credit you deserve.
syntheses.edge_case: This mindset often alienates colleagues over time.
syntheses.forward: Tomorrow, deliberately pass credit over to a teammate for something you both worked on. Watch what happens.

syntheses.narrative: You care deeply about the mission, which makes it incredibly frustrating when the system doesn't reward you or actively blocks your path. You feel owed by an unfair system.
syntheses.edge_case: Waiting for a broken system to be fair will exhaust you.
syntheses.forward: Tomorrow, stop tracking what the organization is failing to give you, and just focus on the mission.

syntheses.narrative: You're attempting to give, but you feel completely tossed around by the system and are internalizing the friction as a personal assault.
syntheses.edge_case: Your empathy is high, but your locus of control is depleted.
syntheses.forward: Tomorrow, draw a hard box around what you can control. Live inside the box.

default: Today you showed a mix of internal and external locus, contribution and entitlement, self-focus and altrocentrism. That's human. What's interesting: which direction do you want to lean tomorrow?

text: Session Complete. See you tomorrow.
